content.text: Platform as a Service (PaaS) is a cloud delivery model in which the cloud provider manages the complete runtime environment — operating system, language runtimes, middleware, scaling, and networking — while you supply only your application code and data. You interact with a
(Git push, CLI upload, container image) rather than with virtual machines or SSH sessions.
On AWS the primary PaaS services are

(full-stack frontend and mobile backends). Managed database services such as RDS, Aurora, and ElastiCache are also PaaS in nature because AWS owns the database engine installation, patching, replication, and failover.

The operational burden gap between IaaS and PaaS is large. With IaaS you provision an EC2 instance and then you are responsible for every patch, every OS update, every runtime version upgrade, and every scaling policy. A team of three engineers can spend a significant fraction of its time on that overhead before writing a single line of application logic.

PaaS removes that overhead. A startup can deploy a production-grade Node.js web application on Elastic Beanstalk in under ten minutes, with TLS termination, Auto Scaling, health monitoring, and zero-downtime rolling deploys all included by default. The same team gets those capabilities without writing CloudFormation, managing systemd units, or planning AMI rotation schedules.
Deploy a web application with a single command. No AMI selection, no VPC subnet planning, no security-group design required.
Elastic Beanstalk and App Runner scale the compute fleet automatically based on request load or CPU metrics, with no Auto Scaling Group configuration needed.
AWS patches the guest OS and runtime. You update your application; AWS handles the platform.
Health dashboards, CloudWatch integration, and log streaming are provisioned automatically. You do not configure the metrics agent.
Fewer engineer hours spent on infrastructure means more time on the product. This advantage compounds at small team sizes.
Elastic Beanstalk keeps a configurable number of application versions. A bad deploy can be rolled back to a prior version in seconds.
Beanstalk-specific configuration files (.ebextensions) or App Runner service definitions are AWS-specific. Migrating to another provider requires rework.

Beanstalk supports a fixed set of managed platforms (Node.js 20, Python 3.12, Java 21, etc.). If your workload requires a non-standard runtime or kernel module, you need a custom platform or a move to IaaS.
PaaS sits on top of IaaS resources. A large, stable workload often costs less on hand-tuned EC2 or containers with reserved capacity than on Beanstalk's on-demand fleet.
The platform makes decisions about instance type, OS, and deployment mechanics. Debugging unusual behavior sometimes requires understanding the platform internals rather than your own code.
Advanced networking (custom NIC placement, SR-IOV tuning, GPU instance families) is either unavailable or requires escaping to Beanstalk worker environments or custom platforms.

The diagram below shows the shared responsibility split for PaaS. Compared to IaaS, the customer-managed zone shrinks to just application code and data. AWS takes ownership of the runtime, OS, scaling, networking, and all physical layers.

attrs.alt: PaaS shared responsibility diagram showing customer-managed and AWS-managed layers
attrs.title: PaaS Shared Responsibility Model

content.text: When you push application code to Elastic Beanstalk, the platform orchestrates a sequence automatically: it provisions or reuses EC2 instances with the correct platform AMI, installs and configures the chosen runtime, deploys your code bundle, configures an Application Load Balancer, sets up health checks, and registers the environment with CloudWatch. None of those steps require commands from you.

Elastic Beanstalk is the oldest and most feature-rich AWS PaaS. You upload a ZIP archive or a Docker image and choose a platform (Node.js, Python, Java, .NET, PHP, Ruby, Go, or a custom Docker platform). Beanstalk creates an environment that consists of an Auto Scaling Group, an Application Load Balancer (for web-tier environments), and optionally an RDS database.
YAML files in your application source bundle. These files can install packages, create files, run commands, and configure Beanstalk environment options. A more modern alternative is the
directory, which uses hooks (pre-build, build, post-build, pre-deploy, post-deploy) to extend the platform lifecycle.

App Runner (launched 2021) is a newer, fully managed service for containerized web applications and APIs. You provide either a container image (from ECR) or source code in a supported runtime (Python, Node.js, Java). App Runner builds and deploys it, manages the compute behind an HTTPS endpoint, and scales down to near-zero when idle. There is no environment configuration file; everything is specified in the service definition or Console.

App Runner is simpler than Beanstalk but has less customization. You cannot control the underlying instance types, configure custom VPC endpoints beyond a VPC connector, or use worker-tier (background job) deployments. It is best for stateless HTTP services that need to get to production quickly.

Amplify targets frontend web and mobile developers. It provides a Git-connected CI/CD pipeline that builds and deploys static frontends (React, Next.js, Vue, Angular) to a global CDN (CloudFront), with branch preview URLs for pull requests. Amplify also offers a backend library (Amplify Studio / Amplify Gen 2) for provisioning authentication (Cognito), data (AppSync + DynamoDB), storage (S3), and serverless functions (Lambda) directly from TypeScript code.

Amazon RDS and Aurora are database PaaS. AWS manages the database engine installation, storage provisioning, automated backups, point-in-time restore, Multi-AZ replication, and read replica promotion. You interact purely at the SQL level: you do not SSH into a database server, configure the PostgreSQL binary, or manually trigger a failover.
Aurora Serverless v2 extends this by automatically scaling database capacity in fine-grained increments based on connection load, making it a true serverless database PaaS for variable workloads.
Choose PaaS when the primary bottleneck is engineering time rather than infrastructure cost, and when your workload fits the platform's supported runtimes.

for web applications and background workers using standard runtimes, particularly when you want the control of IaaS (VPC placement, security group control, instance type selection) with PaaS deployment convenience.
for stateless containerized HTTP APIs that need zero-to-production speed and can tolerate the reduced configurability.
for frontend or full-stack teams that want CI/CD, CDN hosting, and backend infrastructure generated from TypeScript without writing CloudFormation.

when you need specific kernel parameters, custom build toolchains not in the managed platforms, GPU compute, or when unit cost at scale is the primary concern and you have the team to manage EC2 properly.
How to Implement — Elastic Beanstalk CLI Walkthrough
The EB CLI is the primary tool for local Beanstalk development. Install it with
then follow these steps to deploy a Node.js application.

# Create a production environment with an Application Load Balancer
# --elb-type application provisions an ALB instead of Classic ELB
# --instance-types selects the compute tier
eb create production \
  --elb-type application \
  --instance-types t3.small \
  --min-instances 1 \
  --max-instances 4

# Beanstalk provisions: ALB, ASG, EC2 instances, CloudWatch alarms
# Wait for environment to become Green (~3-5 minutes)

# Edit app.js to change the response, then deploy
eb deploy production

# Rolling deploy: replaces instances in batches so there is no downtime
# Status updates stream to the terminal as Beanstalk progresses

# Check the environment URL
eb open

# Stream environment events
eb events --follow

# Roll back to a previous version if the deploy introduced a regression
eb appversion list
eb deploy production --version <label-from-list>

# Set environment properties (available as process.env.* in Node.js)
eb setenv \
  DATABASE_URL=postgresql://mydb.cluster.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/app \
  LOG_LEVEL=info \
  NODE_ENV=production

# For secrets, prefer Secrets Manager references rather than plain-text values:
# In .ebextensions/secrets.config:
# option_settings:
#   aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment:
#     DB_PASSWORD: '{{resolve:secretsmanager:prod/app/dbpass:SecretString:password}}'
How to Implement — AWS App Runner
App Runner uses a service configuration. The example below deploys a containerized Python Flask API from an ECR image.

# Build and push a container image to ECR
AWS_ACCOUNT=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)
REGION=us-east-1
REPO=my-flask-api

aws ecr create-repository --repository-name $REPO --region $REGION

aws ecr get-login-password --region $REGION | \
  docker login --username AWS \
  --password-stdin $AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com

docker build -t $REPO .
docker tag $REPO:latest \
  $AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com/$REPO:latest
docker push $AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com/$REPO:latest
A three-person startup is building a B2B SaaS product with a React frontend and a Node.js REST API. They use the following PaaS stack entirely:
deploys the React frontend from GitHub on every merge to main. Branch preview environments are created automatically for each pull request, giving the product team a live URL to review before merge.
runs the API in a two-instance Auto Scaling group behind an ALB. The team runs
from their CI pipeline (GitHub Actions) after tests pass.
(db.t3.medium, Multi-AZ) stores all application data. AWS manages backups, replication, and failover. The team defines the schema via Flyway migrations.
(managed Redis cluster) handles session tokens and rate limiting counters.

The team's entire AWS infrastructure is 0 EC2 management, 0 OS patches, 0 database failover scripts. Their GitHub Actions pipeline is 32 lines. A single engineer can manage the full infrastructure part-time. When they grow to 20 engineers and 10,000 customers, they re-evaluate whether migrating the API tier to ECS or EKS with more granular autoscaling saves enough money to justify the operational cost increase.
Storing state in the local filesystem.
Beanstalk instances are ephemeral. File uploads written to disk disappear on the next rolling deploy. All persistent data must go to S3, RDS, or EFS — never to local disk.
Putting secrets in environment properties unencrypted.

Beanstalk environment properties are visible in the Console and via API calls to anyone with DescribeEnvironments access. Use Secrets Manager references or Parameter Store SecureString parameters and resolve them at runtime.
All-at-once brings down all instances simultaneously during deployment, causing downtime. Use Rolling with additional batch or Immutable deployments in production.
Running the RDS instance inside Beanstalk's environment.
Beanstalk can create an RDS instance as part of the environment, but then deleting the environment deletes the database. Provision RDS independently and pass the endpoint as an environment variable.

AWS retires Beanstalk platform versions on a schedule. Applications on an EOL platform version stop receiving security patches and eventually cannot be updated. Monitor the platform changelog and schedule regular platform updates.
Overriding too many Beanstalk defaults in .ebextensions.

The more you customize the platform, the more you are effectively building your own IaaS on top of Beanstalk. If you find yourself writing extensive platform hooks, re-evaluate whether ECS or EC2 is a better fit.
Use immutable or traffic-splitting deployments in production.

Immutable deployments create a new Auto Scaling Group, run the new version alongside the old, then shift traffic and terminate the old group. No shared instances are reused, so a failed deploy never corrupts the running environment.
Keep environments isolated by lifecycle stage.
Maintain separate Beanstalk environments for development, staging, and production. They are inexpensive and guarantee that production never shares resources with test traffic.
Application instances must be fully stateless: sessions in ElastiCache/Redis, uploads in S3, data in RDS. This is a prerequisite for any horizontal scaling to work correctly.
Use a custom VPC with private subnets.
Place Beanstalk EC2 instances in private subnets. The ALB goes in public subnets. This prevents direct internet access to application servers.
Attach an IAM instance profile with least-privilege permissions.

Beanstalk creates a default instance profile, but its policies are broad. Replace it with a custom profile that grants only what your application calls (specific S3 buckets, specific Secrets Manager ARNs, specific DynamoDB tables).
Check the managed platform update schedule.

Enable Managed Platform Updates in the Beanstalk environment settings. AWS will apply minor and patch version updates during a configured maintenance window, keeping the platform current without manual effort.
PaaS performance is primarily determined by three choices: instance type, scaling policy, and deployment strategy.

Beanstalk supports all standard EC2 families. Choose memory-optimized (r-series) for Node.js applications with large in-process caches, compute-optimized (c-series) for CPU-bound tasks, and general-purpose (t3/m-series) for typical web workloads.

The default scaling trigger is CPU utilization. For I/O-bound web servers, a better trigger is ALB RequestCountPerTarget — scale on the number of concurrent requests per instance rather than CPU, which may stay low even under load due to async I/O.

Auto Scaling adds capacity reactively. If you have predictable traffic spikes (e.g., Monday morning), use Scheduled Scaling to pre-warm capacity before the spike arrives. This avoids performance degradation during the time it takes for new instances to pass health checks.

App Runner scales on a concurrency model: one instance is added for every N concurrent requests (configurable, default 100). Tune this based on your application's memory footprint per request rather than accepting the default.
— the primary local development and deployment tool for Elastic Beanstalk. Wraps the Beanstalk API in developer-friendly commands:
— define Beanstalk applications and environments as infrastructure as code. The CDK
L1 constructs wrap the CloudFormation resources directly.
GitHub Action combined with the EB CLI handles automated deploys on push to main.
— tracks platform-level events that affect your Beanstalk environments (AMI deprecations, maintenance events, platform retirements).
— analyzes CloudWatch metrics for Beanstalk-managed EC2 instances and recommends right-sized instance types, potentially reducing costs significantly on over-provisioned environments.
PaaS vs IaaS vs FaaS Comparison
Unlimited (request timeout up to ALB limit)
PaaS narrows but does not eliminate your security responsibilities.
AWS patches the OS and runtime, but SQL injection, broken authentication, SSRF, and dependency CVEs in your application code are still your responsibility. Run
in CI and address critical dependency CVEs before deploy.

The Beanstalk instance profile is the credential set that your application code uses when calling AWS APIs. Scope it to the minimum required ARNs. An over-broad profile means a compromised app process can access all your S3 buckets or assume other roles.
Security Groups on the ALB and instances.

The ALB security group should accept inbound 443 from 0.0.0.0/0. The instance security group should accept inbound only from the ALB security group (not from 0.0.0.0/0). This prevents bypassing the load balancer.

Attach an ACM certificate to the ALB listener on port 443. Redirect port 80 to 443 at the ALB level. Traffic between the ALB and EC2 instances is within AWS's private network but use HTTPS there too for defense in depth if handling PII.

Instance Metadata Service v2 requires a PUT request to obtain a session token before metadata can be read. This mitigates SSRF attacks that attempt to steal the instance role credentials from 169.254.169.254.
Think of the three models as three ways to buy transportation.
You get a vehicle and a tank of gas. You choose the route, maintain the tires, fill up when empty, and get traffic tickets.

You tell the driver where to go. The driver manages the route, refuels, handles maintenance, and pays tolls. You still decide the destination (your application logic). You cannot take a sudden detour the driver's route system does not support.
You pay per trip. Zero cost when you are not moving. No car to maintain at all. But you cannot carry a couch (long-running tasks), and you go where taxis go (supported trigger types).
PaaS is the middle point in control versus responsibility. It is the right choice when you want production-grade infrastructure without a dedicated platform team.
PaaS delegates OS, runtime, scaling, and networking to the provider. You own only application code and data.
AWS PaaS services: Elastic Beanstalk (web + worker), App Runner (containerized HTTP APIs), Amplify (frontend + full-stack), RDS/Aurora (database PaaS).
The deployment unit is a code bundle or container image, not a server.
Application instances must be stateless. Persistent data goes to S3, RDS, or ElastiCache, never to local disk.
Use immutable or rolling-with-additional-batch deployments in production to avoid downtime.
Security responsibilities shift but do not disappear: application-layer vulnerabilities, IAM least privilege, Security Group design, secret management, and HTTPS remain yours.
PaaS cost tends to exceed raw IaaS at scale but is justified by reduced ops overhead for teams without dedicated platform engineers.
